goals.description:
Each school site will develop and maintain positive parent, student, and community involvement and
focus on engagement strategies to promote and support student social, emotional, behavioral well-
being and academic success.

actions.title:
Universal Program:
MTSS SEB Program
for Social, Emotional,
and Behavior Well-
Being of All Students
actions.description:
SVUSD will continue to implement and refine a Multi-Tiered System of
Supports for Social, Emotional, and Behavior (MTSS SEB) well-being that
is culturally proficient, supportive, and inclusive.
S V USD MTSS SEB is our version of PBIS, which is an evidence-based
program designed to create a positive, safe, and welcoming learning
environment. Through MTSS SEB, we focus on strategies for teaching and
supporting student social-emotional well-being as well as appropriate
behavior. We will support all schools to develop school-wide and
classroom-wide behavior expectations as part of MTSS SEB (PBIS).
Our MTSS SEB program will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.5 Suspension Rate, M1.6 Expulsion
Rate, and M1.13 ODR.
See Actions 1.4 ($61,597 using LREBG), 1.5 ($89,450 using EEF), 1.6
($72,660 using LCFF Supplemental), 1.23 ($762,854 using EEF) and 1.24
($89,450 using EEF) for more details.

actions.title:
Universal Program:
Second Step Social-
Emotional Learning
Curriculum
actions.description:
We will implement the "Second Step" Social-Emotional Learning
Curriculum across TK-8. This program equips students with essential skills
to manage emotions, set positive goals, show empathy for others, and
make responsible decisions.
This curriculum supports a well-rounded educational experience for
everyone, and is especially important for our foster youth, English learners,
and low-income students, to ensure that they have a safe and respectful
learning environment.
Second Step will contribute to student outcomes as measured by metric
M1.9 Self-Management, Social Awareness, and Self-Efficacy.
Cost is accounted for in Goal 3 Action 3.1, instructional materials adoption
($144,943), and is not included here.

actions.title:
Universal Program:
Digital Citizenship
Program for All
Students
actions.description:
SVUSD will refine and enhance the Digital Citizenship Program in order to
help our students be safe, responsible, and ethical digital citizens. We
recognize that technologies, such as social media and artificial intelligence,
affect students’ social-emotional and behavioral well-being. Our goal is to
make sure all students can use the technologies of today and the future in
a way that’s safe, responsible, and ethical.
Our Digital Citizenship Program will contribute to student outcomes as
measured by metrics M1.5 Suspension Rate and M1.6 Expulsion Rate.
The Digital Citizenship Program is a part of our Instruction Design and
Technology program (Goal 2 Action 2.38). Cost ($113,071) is accounted
for in Goal 2 Action 2.38 and is not included here.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
School Climate
Assessment and
Progress Monitoring
actions.description:
SVUSD will use a school climate survey to help us assess, diagnose, and
monitor progress in student engagement and sense of belonging as part of
the intervention component of our MTSS SEB. It will also be used to
monitor progress on students’ self-management, social awareness, and
s e lf-efficacy as part of social-emotional learning. Currently, we are using
Panorama as the school climate survey.
This is especially important to help us understand and focus social-
emotional and behavior support for our students, especially our foster
youth, English learners, and low-income students. Our data indicates that
our foster youth, English learners, and low-income students experience
increased barriers to social-emotional well-being and sense of belonging.
Assessing and monitoring our school climate will contribute to student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.4 SEL Belonging and Engagement
and M1.9 SEL Self-management, Social Awareness, and Self-Efficacy.
This action addresses the requirements for LREBG. For 2025-26, LREBG
provides $61,597 to fund this action. Research shows that a strong MTSS,
including a comprehensive assessment system, supports SEL and student
positive behavior (What Works Clearinghouse IES, 2020).

actions.title:
Intervention System:
MTSS SEB School
Teams
actions.description:
We will have MTSS SEB School Teams, which include teachers, the
administrator, and one parent/guardian member. These teams meet
regularly to receive professional development, analyze student data,
monitor progress, and plan intervention strategies and recommendations
for school staff as part of our MTSS SEB framework. The MTSS SEB
School Teams will focus on providing additional support to students to help
with their social-emotional and behavioral well-being.
This ongoing professional learning and collaboration is especially important
for improving social-emotional and behavioral supports for our foster youth,
English learners, and low-income students.
Our MTSS SEB School Teams will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.5 Suspension Rate, M1.6 Expulsion
Rate, and M1.13 ODR.
The $89,450.00 in cost, as detailed in Action 1.24, is funded through
Educator Effectiveness Funds (EEF) because each session begins with
structured professional development to strengthen staff skills in positive
social, emotional, and behavioral practices.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Student Study Team
actions.description:
Our Student Study Team (SST) helps us understand and meet the
i n d ividual social, emotional, and behavioral needs of each student through
a detailed, personalized plan, developed through the SST process or the
504 process. We bring together teachers, parents/guardians, and, if
needed, specialists to plan how best to support each student's learning and
growth at school.
The Student Study Team (SST) is an important component of MTSS SEB.
The SST supports our students with a more personalized plan beyond
what the Tier 1 MTSS SEB school teams can recommend, which is
particularly important for our foster youth, English learners, and low-
income students. Our data shows that our English learners and low
income students are referred at a higher rate than all students for socio-
emotional, behavioral concerns.
Our Student Study Team will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.5 Suspension Rate and M1.9 SEL
Self-management, Social Awareness, and Self-efficacy. We will measure
implementation with M1.12 SST SEB and M1.13 ODR.
Please see Goal 2 Action 2.15 for more information on how the SST
supports academic needs.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Elementary
Counseling Program
actions.description:
Our elementary school counselors help our children grow into well-rounded
individuals who feel safe and supported. They help develop important life
s k ills such as empathy, emotional regulation, and problem-solving through
classroom lessons, targeted small group skill-building workshops, and
individual counseling. At times, they provide immediate crisis intervention
for mental health or emotional distress.
The elementary school counselors will contribute to improvement in
student outcomes as measured by metric M1.9 SEL Self-management,
Social Awareness, and Self-efficacy.
We will develop a metric to monitor implementation of the elementary
counseling program in 2024-25.
This action addresses the requirements for LREBG. For 2025-26, LREBG
provides $982,929 to fund this action. Research shows that
comprehensive school counseling programs align with theories of student
support that emphasize the role of counselors in promoting academic
achievement and social-emotional development, particularly for at-risk
student groups (American School Counselor Association, 2019, WWC,
2020).

Intervention System:
Supplemental
Elementary
Counselors at Title-1
Schools
actions.description:
To further enhance our support for our low-income students, we will make
sure that our elementary Title-I schools have two additional days of
counseling since our Title-1 school sites have a higher percentage of low-
income students and our data shows that they face higher barriers to
social-emotional well-being.
The additional days of counseling at Title-I schools will contribute to
improvement in student outcomes as measured by metric M1.9 SEL Self-
management, Social Awareness, and Self-efficacy.
We will develop a metric to monitor implementation of the elementary
counseling program in 2024-25.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Secondary
Counseling Program
actions.description:
Secondary school counselors provide a comprehensive school counseling
program that supports the academic success, post-secondary college and
career readiness and social development of our students. Secondary
counselors provide classroom guidance lessons, targeted small group
workshops and individual counseling services that support the whole child
and help each student to reach their fullest potential.
Counselors are available to all students, with a particular focus on foster
youth, English learners, and low-income students.
The secondary counselors will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.1 Graduation Rate, M1.5 ODR, and
M1.9 SEL Self-management, Social Awareness, and Self-efficacy. We will
measure implementation with M1.14 Counseling Services.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Mental Wellness
Counseling Program
actions.description:
Mental Wellness Counselors provide short-term individual counseling
services for TK - 12th grade students across the district through a
c e ntralized referral process. The program is designed to support students
experiencing depression, anxiety and other mental health related concerns
that are impacting the student’s ability to be successful at school. This
intensive intervention is designed to improve student mental health and
make positive changes to attendance, academics, behavior and social-
emotional well-being.
This support is available to all students, with a particular focus on foster
youth, English learners, and low-income students.
The mental wellness counselors will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.9 SEL Self-management, Social
Awareness, and Self-efficacy.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Attendance
actions.description:
We are committed to improving our attendance. One way we will do this is
by discussing attendance with parents and families during existing
meetings, such as parent-teacher conferences, Student Study Team
meetings, and IEP meetings. If needed, we will create goals and action
plans during these meetings. We will also share attendance information in
large group settings through flyers, posters, and at Back to School Night.
To further improve student attendance, we will create systems to identify
students who frequently miss school as soon as possible and address the
underlying causes of their absences. We will work closely with students
and their families to provide the necessary resources and support,
ensuring that students have the encouragement and assistance they need
to attend school regularly. By focusing on consistent attendance, we aim to
enhance academic performance and increase opportunities for student
success.
The attendance intervention system will contribute to improvement in
student outcomes as measured by metrics M1.4 Chronic Absenteeism and
M1.11 Attendance Rates.
Attendance intervention is part of the work of the Student Services
department (Goal 1 Action 1.25). Cost ($638,002.00) is accounted for as
part of Goal 1 Action 1.25 and is not included here.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Alternative to
Suspension (ATS)
actions.description:
We will continue to implement an alternative to suspension (ATS) program
at the secondary level, focusing on constructive interventions that address
behavior while keeping students learning.
This approach is particularly beneficial for foster youth, English learners,
and low-income students, as students’ learning is not interrupted. By
replacing suspensions with supportive measures, the program aims to
reduce disruptions in education and promote better long-term outcomes.
Alternative to suspension will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metric M1.5 Suspensions.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Many Routes to
Graduation
actions.description:
SVUSD provides multiple pathways to graduation, ensuring that all
students can succeed and earn their diplomas in a way that best fits their
needs. This includes taking advantage of state exemptions and other
alternative pathways that accommodate different situations. A committee
meets on a regular basis to monitor and review situations where students
might qualify for an alternative pathway.
Many routes to graduation will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metric M1.1 Grad Rate.
The work of providing multiple pathways to graduation is a collaboration
between two departments. Cost is accounted for as part of Goal 1 Action
1.25, Student Services department ($638,002.00) and Goal 2 Action 2.37,
Secondary MTSS Academics department ($450,318.00), and is not
included here.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Transcript
Translation
actions.description:
We will offer transcript translation services to assist students from other
countries by accurately translating their academic records into English.
This service is important for our English learners as it ensures that they
receive appropriate credit for previous coursework completed in different
languages and helps make sure that students graduate in a timely manner.
Transcript translations will contribute to improvement in student outcomes
as measured by metric M1.1 Grad Rate.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Secondary
Intervention
actions.description:
We will continue to offer secondary intervention to provide targeted support
for students who need additional help in specific subjects. Interventions
may include additional sections designed to offer more personalized
attention and tailored instruction, enabling students to grasp challenging
concepts and improve their academic performance.
The secondary interventions will help students complete the credits
needed to graduate, contributing to improvement in student outcomes as
measured by both metric M1.1 Grad Rate and metric M1.16 Completed
Credits.
This action addresses the requirements for LREBG. For 2025-26, LREBG
provides $407,823 to fund this action. Research shows that a strong
MTSS, including data-based interventions, supports positive academic
student outcomes (What Works Clearinghouse IES, 2020).

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Credit Recovery
actions.description:
SVUSD offers a credit recovery program for secondary students to regain
missed credits and stay on track for graduation. Credit recovery includes
access to online courses provided in collaboration with other educational
institutions, offering a flexible and accessible way to complete necessary
coursework.
Credit recovery is available for all students, and is especially helpful for
foster youth, English learners, and low-income students as our data shows
that they have a lower graduation rate.
Credit recovery will contribute to improvement in student outcomes as
measured by metric M1.1 Grad Rate. We will monitor implementation using
metric M1.16 Completed Credits.

actions.title:
Intervention System:
Secondary Summer
School
actions.description:
We will continue to provide the secondary summer school program,
enabling students to recover credits, take additional elective courses to
meet graduation requirements, accelerate, and/or receive intervention.
Through summer school, students receive the guidance and academic
support needed to successfully complete their courses.
Credit recovery during summer school is available to all students, but is
especially important for our foster youth, English learners, and low-income
students as our data shows that they have a lower graduation rate.
This will contribute to improving student outcomes in metric M1.1 Grad
Rate. We will monitor implementation with metric M1.16 Completed
Credits.

actions.title:
Robust Professional
Development
Program:
Cultural Proficiency
actions.description:
We are committed to a welcoming and inclusive school culture. We will
offer ongoing professional development in culturally proficient practices
and strategies to all of our staff to help them create learning environments
that support each and every student and connect with every family. A
welcoming and inclusive school culture is important for everyone,
especially our foster youth, English learners, and low-income students.
Professional development on culturally proficient practices and strategies
will contribute to improvement in student outcomes as measured by
metrics M1.4 Engagement and Sense of Belonging. It will also contribute to
improvement in our parent engagement as measured by metric M1.7
Parent Engagement and M1.8 Parent Survey.

actions.title:
Robust Professional
Development
Program:
Engagement
Strategies
actions.description:
SVUSD will provide ongoing professional development on student
engagement strategies so that schools are well equipped to build an
engaging and welcoming learning environment. The professional
development will include strategies for how to make learning motivational
and meaningful in different ways by engaging students cognitively, socially,
and emotionally in their learning.
Professional development on engagement strategies will contribute to
improvement in student outcomes as measured by metrics M1.4
Engagement and Sense of Belonging.
Cost is accounted for using other funding sources and is not included here.

actions.title:
Robust Professional
Development
Program:
Social-Emotional
Learning
actions.description:
We will offer ongoing professional development in social-emotional
learning to all of our staff to help them create supportive classrooms that
nurture students' emotional well-being alongside their academic learning.
Through social-emotional learning, teachers help students develop skills in
understanding and managing emotions, setting positive goals, showing
empathy for others, and maintaining positive relationships.
Professional development on social-emotional learning will contribute to
improvement in student outcomes as measured by metric M1.4
Engagement and Sense of Belonging and M1.9 Self-Management, Social
Awareness, and Self-Efficacy.

actions.title:
Robust Professional
Development
Program:
Community
Circles/Restorative
Practices
actions.description:
We will provide ongoing professional development in community circles
and restorative practices so that our teachers and school staff can build an
inclusive community that supports a sense of belonging. Once a sense of
belonging has developed, community circles can become a place that
helps resolve conflicts respectfully.
Professional development on community circles and restorative practices
work alongside our MTSS SEB to provide social-emotional and behavior
support and build a welcoming school culture for our students. This is
helpful for all students, especially our foster youth, English learners, and
low-income students.
This will contribute to improvement in student outcomes as measured by
metrics M1.4 Engagement and Sense of Belonging.
Cost ($5,000) is accounted for using Educator Effectiveness Fund.

actions.title:
Robust Professional
Development:
Leadership Academy
actions.description:
We will provide professional development to our school leadership teams,
which include teachers and administrators, to learn about and develop
c u l turally proficient school practices. During these sessions, they will look
at school data to help plan better ways to support student success and
develop a school culture that is welcoming and inclusive. This training
brings school leaders together to share ideas on how to remove barriers to
learning.
Supporting our school leadership teams to develop a welcoming school
culture supports the social-emotional, behavioral, and academic success of
each of our students, especially our foster youth, English learners, and
low-income students.
Professional development for our school leadership team will contribute to
improvement in student outcomes as measured by metrics M1.4
Engagement and Sense of Belonging. It will also contribute to
improvement in parent engagement as measured by metric M1.7 Parent
Engagement and M1.8 Parent Survey.

actions.title:
Robust Professional
Development
Program: MTSS
TOSA for SEB
actions.description:
The teachers on special assignment (TOSAs) for MTSS SEB will provide
district-wide professional development on effective social-emotional,
behavioral strategies and support the SVUSD MTSS SEB, which is our
version of PBIS. The TOSAs will lead professional development so that all
schools can define and reinforce expected behaviors, monitor and respond
to unexpected behaviors, and use data to determine next steps.
Ongoing professional development on MTSS SEB will promote positive
behavior in the classroom in a proactive manner.
The work that the MTSS SEB TOSAs do will contribute to student
outcomes in many metrics, especially metrics M1.5 Suspension Rate and
M1.6 Expulsion Rate.

actions.title:
Robust Professional
Development
Program:
MTSS SEB
Facilitators
actions.description:
We will have MTSS SEB facilitators plan and provide ongoing professional
development with the MTSS SEB teams for their school staff. MTSS SEB
facilitators are classroom teachers who are a part of the SEB teams. The
professional development will focus on what teachers can do in the
classroom to define and reinforce expected behaviors, monitor and
respond to unexpected behaviors, and use data to determine next steps.
This is especially important to help us provide culturally proficient social-
emotional and behavior support for our students, especially our foster
youth, English learners, and low-income students.
Our MTSS SEB school facilitators will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.5 Suspension Rate and M1.6
Expulsion Rate.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Student Services
Department
actions.description:
SVUSD will maintain a coordinated and comprehensive infrastructure
through the Student Services department to support the actions in Goal 1,
including programs such as MTSS SEB, attendance, and counseling.
Student Services is necessary in order to ensure that the different
components of the intervention system and robust professional
development for social-emotional and behavior well-being are done well, in
accordance with evidence-based practices.
Student Services supports all students, with a particular focus on foster
youth, English learners, and low-income students.
The Student Services Department and staff will contribute to improvement
in student outcomes as measured by many metrics, especially M1.4
Engagement and Sense of Belonging, M1.5 Suspension Rate, and M1.13
ODR.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Secondary
Orientation
actions.description:
We will provide incoming sixth and ninth grade students with an orientation
program, fostering a welcoming and inclusive school climate for all
students. The orientation is especially important for foster youth, English
learners, and low-income students, offering them essential support and
resources as they transition into unfamiliar educational settings.
Orientation helps bridge cultural differences and ensures all students start
with the same knowledge and confidence needed for success in their new
school community.
Orientation will contribute to student outcomes as measured by metrics
M1.4 Sense of Belonging.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Homeless Youth
Support
actions.description:
All Homeless Youth are unduplicated and fall within the low-income
student group. The supports they deserve are unique, varied, and require
targeted responses.
The support we have for housing insecure/homeless youth ensures our
students are properly identified by school personnel and supported through
coordination activities with other entities and agencies. We ensure prompt
student enrollment, transfer of all records and credits, and that students
have a full and equal opportunity to succeed in all of our schools. The
support also ensures that students receive appropriate school-based
services, such as counseling and health services, supplemental instruction,
and after-school services.
Our homeless youth support includes the Mckinney-Vento (MV) Liaison
and Student Champions. The Liaison is a critical role to ensure our
housing insecure/homeless youth receive coordinated support and
services arising from their unique needs. The Student Champions are
teachers and classified staff at the schools who check in with the students
and connect them with district resources. This action addresses our
technical assistance requirement.
Homeless youth support will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.1 Grad Rate, M1.2 Dropout, M1.3
Chronic Absenteeism, M1.5 Suspension Rate, M2.1 ELA and M2.5 Math
for housing insecure/homeless youth.

actions.title:
Family Engagement:
Community
Partnership
actions.description:
Regularly occurring events, communication and school partnerships such
as PTA and PTSO play a vital role in our district's focus to strengthen the
partnership between our schools and the families our schools serve. A
thriving partnership promotes a safe, inclusive and welcoming school
environment and therefore has a direct impact on student learning. This
action aims to ensure that all families feel connected to their children’s
education and that all of their voices are heard, valued and inform the
efforts to continually improve our schools.
Community partnership will contribute to improvement in family
engagement as measured by metrics M1.7 Parent Engagement and M1.8
Parent Survey.
This is a statement of intent for multiple actions, including site-based
events using school site allocations ($145,964.00 under Goal 3 Action 3.6)
and PTA/PTSO funded activities.

actions.title:
Family Engagement:
Communication &
Engagement Tools
actions.description:
We will supplement family communication by using infographics, posters,
and videos to convey important information in a visually engaging way.
T his approach ensures that all families can easily understand school
policies, events, and updates.
Clear communication with families is important for all, but is especially
important for our foster youth, English learners, and low-income students
to ensure everyone has access to the same information.
Communication tools will contribute to improvement in family engagement
as measured by metrics M1.7 Parent Engagement and M1.8 Parent
Survey. We wanted to monitor implementation with metric M1.10 Parent
Participation, to be developed in 2024-25. However, the metric turned out
to be more complex and is delayed until 2025-26.

actions.title:
Family Engagement:
Improve
Communication with
Multilingual Families
actions.description:
We will improve communication with multilingual families. This includes
supporting the district and school English Learner Advisory Committee
(ELAC), language interpretation, and other supplemental support that
im proves communication with multilingual families. This ensures that all
families can fully participate in their children's education and school
activities.
Improving communication with multilingual families will contribute to
improvement in family engagement as measured by metrics M1.7 Parent
Engagement and M1.8 Parent Survey. We will monitor implementation with
metric M1.10 Parent Participation.

actions.title:
Family Engagement:
District
Translator/Interpreter
actions.description:
We will continue to have a district translator/interpreter who provides
interpretation and translation services. The district translator ensures that
i m p ortant information such as enrollment forms, newsletters, and policy
updates are available to all, fostering an inclusive and supportive school
environment. This service supports clear communication, helping families
stay informed and engaged in their children's education.
A district translator/interpreter will contribute to improvement in family
engagement as measured by metrics M1.7 Parent Engagement and M1.8
Parent Survey.

actions.title:
Family Engagement:
School Community
Outreach Liaisons
(SCOLs)
actions.description:
We will build strong connections between schools and families by
employing School Community Outreach Liaisons. These liaisons work
directly with families to ensure they feel welcomed, supported, and
included in the school community. They facilitate communication between
home and school, help families navigate educational systems, and connect
them with resources.
Their services are available to families of English learners.
School Community Outreach Liaisons will contribute to improvement in
family engagement as measured by metrics M1.7 Parent Engagement and
M1.8 Parent Survey.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Supplemental Mental
Health Counseling
actions.description:
Mental Wellness counselors provide short-term individual counseling
services for TK - 12th grade students across the district through a
centralized referral process. The program is designed to support students
experiencing depression, anxiety and other mental health related concerns
that are impacting the student’s ability to be successful at school.
Supplemental mental health counseling will have a particular focus on
students with disabilities.
The mental wellness counselors will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.9 SEL Self-management, Social
Awareness, and Self-efficacy for students with disabilities.
Cost ($1,956,330.00) is accounted for as part of Goal 1 Action 1.36,
Supplemental Secondary Counseling, and is not included here.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Unified PE
actions.description:
We will continue to provide a Unified Physical Education (Unified PE)
program where students from special education and general education
learn and grow together. This inclusive approach enriches the school
community by celebrating all abilities, promoting teamwork, and building a
sense of belonging among all students.
Unified PE will contribute to student outcomes as measured by metric M1.4
SEL Sense of Belonging and Engagement.
Cost is accounted for using general fund. The estimated 6 sections for
2025-26 is $130,200 at $21,700 per section.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Supplemental
Cultural Proficiency
Professional
Development
actions.description:
We will provide supplemental professional development on culturally
proficient practices and strategies for our staff across all departments
beyond our school teams (See Goal 1 Action 1.18). For example, this may
include our management teams, front office staff, maintenance and
operations, health clerks, and many others. We recognize that a district-
wide culture that is welcoming and inclusive is essential for full community
participation and educational partnership. A welcoming and inclusive
culture is important for everyone, particularly our foster youth, English
learners, and low-income students.
Professional development on culturally proficient practices will contribute to
improvement in student outcomes as measured by metrics M1.4
Engagement and Sense of Belonging.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Supplemental
Secondary
Counseling
actions.description:
We will provide additional secondary school counselors that support the
academic success, post-secondary college and career readiness, social
development, and mental health well-being of our students.
Counselors are available to all students, with a particular focus on foster
youth, English learners, and low-income students.
The secondary counselors will contribute to improvement in student
outcomes as measured by metrics M1.1 Graduation Rate and M1.9 SEL
Self-management, Social Awareness, and Self-efficacy.
This action addresses the requirements for LREBG. For 2025-26, LREBG
provides $1,956,330 to fund this action. Research shows that
comprehensive school counseling programs align with theories of student
support that emphasize the role of counselors in promoting academic
achievement and social-emotional development, particularly for at-risk
student groups (American School Counselor Association, 2019, WWC,
2020).

actions.title:
Family Engagement:
Promote Parent
Participation
actions.description:
To promote parental participation in programs for foster youth, English
learners, and low-income students, our district will host regular family
engagement nights and informational sessions to help families navigate
the educational system and access resources tailored to their needs. We
will foster active involvement in DELAC/ELAC, School Site Council, and
Superintendent's Parent Advisory, where parents can collaborate with
educators and community members to support their children’s education.
Additionally, we will maintain open lines of communication through email
newsletters and the district parent portal, ensuring a supportive and
inclusive environment for all families.
Promoting parental participation in school programs will enhance our
partnership with all families, particularly families of foster youth, English
learners, and low-income students.
Promoting parent participation in programs for foster youth, English
learners, and low-income students will contribute to improvement in family
engagement as measured by metrics M1.7 Parent Engagement, M1.8
Parent Survey, and M1.10 Parent Participation.
This action was changed from contributing to non-contributing since it is a
statement of intent, with any relevant costs listed in Actions 1.29
($14,965.00), 1.30 ($11,597.00), 1.31 ($94,850.00), and 1.32
($1,268,130.00).

actions.title:
Family Engagement:
Promote Parent
Participation in
Programs for
Students with
Disabilities
actions.description:
To promote parental participation in programs for students with disabilities,
our district will host regular workshops and informational sessions to help
families navigate the educational system and access available resources.
We will also support parents to engage in the Community Advisory
Committee (CAC), ensuring their voices are heard in the decision-making
process. CAC is a group that includes parents, educators, and community
members working together to support special education. Additionally, we
will maintain consistent communication through email newsletters, district
parent portal, and postcards sent by teachers to build family-school
connection.
Promoting parent participation in programs for students with disabilities will
contribute to improvement in family engagement as measured by metrics
M1.7 Parent Engagement, M1.8 Parent Survey, and M1.10 Parent
Participation.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Foster Youth Support
actions.description:
The support we have for foster youth ensures our students are properly
i d e ntified by school personnel and supported through coordination
activities with other entities and agencies. We ensure prompt student
enrollment, transfer of all records and credits, and that students have a full
and equal opportunity to succeed in all of our schools. The support also
ensures that students receive appropriate school-based services, such as
counseling and health services, supplemental instruction, and after-school
services, as well as meeting unique needs like transportation.
Foster youth support will contribute to improvement in student outcomes as
measured by metrics M1.1 Grad Rate, M1.2 Dropout, M1.3 Chronic
Absenteeism, M1.5 Suspension Rate, M2.1 ELA and M2.5 Math for foster
youth.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Transition Support for
Kindergarten
actions.description:
The district provides extra adults in Kindergarten classrooms during the
first five days of school by using substitutes. This helps reduce the number
of students per adult, giving young children more support as they learn
routines and adjust to school. With more hands to help, teachers can give
extra attention to students who may need help feeling comfortable,
especially English learners, foster youth, and low-income students. By
making the transition smoother, students feel safe, confident, and ready to
learn from the start.
Transition support will contribute to improvement in our student outcomes
in M1.11 Attendance and M1.13 Office Discipline Referral for
Kindergarteners.
This action addresses the requirements for LREBG. For 2025-26, LREBG
provides $56,602 to fund this action. The Hun School of Princeton (2019)
indicates the significance of student-teacher ratios in educational settings.

actions.title:
Targeted Program:
Additional SEL &
Behavior Support for
SWD
actions.description:
The district provides extra social-emotional learning (SEL) and behavior
support for students with disabilities (SWD) to help them succeed in
school. Behavior Specialists work closely with students to teach positive
behaviors, coping skills, and strategies for managing emotions. Teachers
and staff receive training on how to support students' social and emotional
needs, creating a safe and welcoming learning environment. By
strengthening SEL and behavior support, students build confidence,
improve relationships, and stay engaged in learning.
Additional SEL and behavior support will contribute to improvement in our
student outcomes M1.11 Attendance and M1.13 Office Discipline Referral
for SWD.
This action addresses the requirements for LREBG. For 2025-26, LREBG
provides $160,313 to fund this action. Research shows that targeted
interventions for SWD can be effective with an effect size of 0.77 (Hattie,
2018).

goals.description:
All students will demonstrate proficiency, or more, with grade level standards and graduate prepared
to be successful in college and career.
